title: The conductor and the metronome
summary: At a Warsaw rehearsal, the new conductor wants strict tempo; the senior cellist wants the orchestra to breathe. Both are defending music.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: The Warsaw Philharmonic is rehearsing Brahms. Their new music director, Anika Müller, is thirty-eight. The principal cellist, Janusz Wójcik, is sixty-two. He has played with this orchestra for thirty years. They have stopped at the slow movement. Anika has just asked him to play 'more strictly'.

body.speaker: Janusz
body.text: I would like to ask, with respect, what you mean by strictly. I am not slow. I checked against the metronome before rehearsal. I am within two beats per minute of the marking.

body.speaker: Anika
body.text: You are within the marking on the bar level. But you are stretching the second beat in every bar by almost a fifth. The audience cannot feel a pulse. We sound like we are apologising for the music.

body.speaker: Janusz
body.text: I am stretching the second beat because the harmony asks for it. Brahms wrote this passage so that the second beat lands on the most painful chord in the movement. If I do not give that chord a moment, the listener never feels it. The metronome does not know the chord is there.

body.speaker: Anika
body.text: I take that point. But you cannot decide alone where to stretch. The brass behind you are following you, and they are not playing the same chord. They are playing a steady rhythm under the harmony. When you breathe, they have to choose between following you, which kills their rhythm, or ignoring you, which breaks the ensemble. You are putting the section in an impossible spot.

body.speaker: Janusz
body.text: I have played this passage in this hall under four conductors. Two of them asked me to stretch. The others did not. It can be done.

body.speaker: Anika
body.text: It can be done by an orchestra that already knows your stretch. The brass in 1998 had played it with you for a decade. The brass in row eight today are three months old in this orchestra. Two of them are still finding their breath. If we do it your way, today, they will not know when to come in. Tomorrow they will know, but tomorrow is not the concert.

body.speaker: Janusz
body.text: So you are saying I should play less honestly for the sake of newer colleagues.

body.speaker: Anika
body.text: I am saying you should play differently when the room is different. You played with old friends and you read each other in your sleep. You are not playing with old friends now. The orchestra has changed. The honest playing in 1998 is not the honest playing in 2024.

body.speaker: Janusz
body.text: That is a fair sentence. But may I offer one in return? An orchestra that is afraid to breathe is not an orchestra. It is a clock. If every conductor for the next ten years protects the new brass by forbidding the breath, the brass will never learn it. They will become a section that cannot follow a cellist. That is also a kind of loss.

body.speaker: Anika
body.text: Then could we try this. Today, you keep the bar steady. We rehearse it without the stretch for the next two days. Then on Saturday, the day before the concert, we rehearse the same passage twice — once steady, once with your stretch — and the brass play both. We see what they can manage. If they can manage the breath, we use the breath in the concert.

body.speaker: Janusz
body.text: Yes. I will play it steady today. I would also like to spend twenty minutes on Friday with the brass, if they agree. Off the clock. We can talk about where I breathe and why.

body.speaker: Anika
body.text: Please do that. And thank you for not just doing it your way.

body.speaker: Janusz
body.text: Thank you for not just calling it wrong.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: On the Saturday rehearsal the brass managed the breath at the second attempt. The Sunday concert was reviewed in two newspapers. One critic called the slow movement 'unusually patient'. The other called it 'almost reckless in its silence'. Neither mentioned the brass, who, Anika later said, were the people the review should have been about.

annotations.meaningVi: giám đốc âm nhạc / nhạc trưởng chính
nhịp đập (cảm giác chung của bài)
sự đồng bộ của dàn nhạc
chơi ít trung thực hơn (với cảm xúc)
đồng hồ (chỉ máy móc, vô hồn)
ngoài giờ trả lương / ngoài giờ chính thức

grammarNotes.title: Conditionals (1st and 2nd) for plans and hypotheses
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng câu điều kiện loại 1 (sự thật / khả năng) và loại 2 (giả định). Trong bài: 'If we do it your way, today, they will not know when to come in.' (1st — kết quả thực tế). 'If they can manage the breath, we use the breath in the concert.' (1st — kế hoạch điều kiện). 'If every conductor for the next ten years protects the new brass by forbidding the breath, the brass will never learn it.' (1st — dự báo dài hạn). So sánh với hypothetical 'would': 'If I do not give that chord a moment, the listener never feels it.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 1st conditional (real / likely) and 2nd conditional (hypothetical). In the text: 'If we do it your way, today, they will not know when to come in.' (1st — likely consequence). 'If they can manage the breath, we use the breath in the concert.' (1st — conditional plan). 'If every conductor for the next ten years protects the new brass by forbidding the breath, the brass will never learn it.' (1st — long-term prediction). Compare hypothetical 'would'-style framing: 'If I do not give that chord a moment, the listener never feels it.'

translationVi:
Dàn nhạc giao hưởng Warsaw đang tập Brahms. Giám đốc âm nhạc mới, Anika Müller, ba mươi tám. Cellist chính, Janusz Wójcik, sáu mươi hai. Ông chơi với dàn này ba mươi năm. Họ đã dừng ở chương chậm. Anika vừa yêu cầu ông chơi 'nghiêm khắc hơn'.

Janusz: Tôi xin phép hỏi, với sự tôn trọng, 'nghiêm khắc' nghĩa là gì. Tôi không chậm. Tôi đã kiểm bằng máy đếm nhịp trước buổi tập. Tôi nằm trong khoảng hai BPM so với chỉ định.

Anika: Ông nằm trong chỉ định ở cấp ô nhịp. Nhưng ông kéo dài nhịp thứ hai trong mỗi ô gần một phần năm. Khán giả không cảm được nhịp đập. Chúng ta nghe như đang xin lỗi vì bản nhạc.

Janusz: Tôi kéo dài nhịp hai vì hoà thanh đòi hỏi. Brahms viết đoạn này để nhịp hai rơi vào hợp âm đau nhất chương. Nếu tôi không cho hợp âm đó một khoảnh khắc, người nghe không bao giờ cảm. Máy đếm nhịp không biết có hợp âm ở đó.

Anika: Tôi nhận điểm đó. Nhưng ông không thể tự quyết kéo ở đâu. Bộ kèn đồng sau ông theo ông, và họ không chơi cùng hợp âm. Họ chơi nhịp đều dưới hoà thanh. Khi ông 'thở', họ phải chọn giữa theo ông — phá nhịp họ — hay phớt ông — vỡ ensemble. Ông đặt bộ kèn vào tình thế bất khả.

Janusz: Tôi đã chơi đoạn này dưới bốn nhạc trưởng. Hai người bảo tôi kéo. Hai người không. Việc đó làm được.

Anika: Bởi một dàn đã biết kéo của ông. Bộ kèn năm 1998 đã chơi với ông một thập kỷ. Bộ kèn hàng tám hôm nay mới ba tháng trong dàn. Hai người vẫn đang tìm hơi. Nếu hôm nay làm theo cách ông, họ không biết vào lúc nào. Mai họ biết, nhưng mai không phải buổi diễn.

Janusz: Vậy bà nói tôi nên chơi ít trung thực hơn vì đồng nghiệp mới.

Anika: Tôi nói ông nên chơi khác khi phòng khác. Ông đã chơi với bạn cũ và đọc nhau như trong mơ. Bây giờ ông không chơi với bạn cũ. Dàn đã đổi. 'Chơi trung thực' năm 1998 không phải 'chơi trung thực' năm 2024.

Janusz: Câu đó công bằng. Nhưng xin tặng lại một câu. Một dàn nhạc sợ thở không phải dàn nhạc. Đó là cái đồng hồ. Nếu mọi nhạc trưởng mười năm tới đều bảo vệ bộ kèn mới bằng cách cấm thở, bộ kèn sẽ không bao giờ học thở. Họ sẽ thành đoạn không thể theo một cellist. Đó cũng là mất mát.

Anika: Vậy ta thử thế này. Hôm nay ông giữ nhịp đều. Hai ngày tới ta tập không kéo. Rồi thứ Bảy, ngày trước concert, ta tập đoạn đó hai lần — một lần đều, một lần có kéo của ông — bộ kèn chơi cả hai. Xem họ làm được không. Nếu họ làm được 'thở', ta dùng 'thở' trong concert.

Janusz: Vâng. Hôm nay tôi chơi đều. Thứ Sáu tôi muốn dành hai mươi phút với bộ kèn nếu họ đồng ý. Ngoài giờ. Bàn về việc tôi 'thở' ở đâu và vì sao.

Anika: Xin ông làm vậy. Và cảm ơn ông đã không chỉ làm theo ý mình.

Janusz: Cảm ơn bà đã không chỉ gọi nó là sai.

Thứ Bảy bộ kèn 'thở' được ở lượt thứ hai. Buổi diễn Chủ Nhật được hai báo điểm. Một nhà phê bình gọi chương chậm là 'kiên nhẫn bất thường'. Người kia gọi là 'gần như liều lĩnh trong sự im lặng'. Không ai nhắc bộ kèn, mà theo Anika sau này, đáng ra bài viết nên về họ.

options: Anika Müller, the new music director (38); Janusz Wójcik, the principal cellist (62, 30 years in the orchestra)

questions.prompt: What does Anika first complain about?
options: Janusz is stretching the second beat by almost a fifth; audience cannot feel a pulse

questions.prompt: How does Janusz defend the stretch?
options: The second beat lands on the most painful chord; without a moment, the listener never feels it; the metronome does not know the chord is there

questions.prompt: Why does Anika say the brass section is in trouble?
options: Brass play a steady rhythm under the harmony; if he breathes they must choose to follow (kill rhythm) or ignore (break ensemble) — impossible spot

questions.prompt: Why is this orchestra different from 1998?
options: Today's brass are only 3 months old in the orchestra; two are still finding their breath
Honest playing in 1998 is not the same as honest playing in 2024 — different room, different orchestra

questions.prompt: What is Janusz's counter-argument about long-term protection?
options: If conductors forbid the breath to protect new brass, the brass will never learn it; they'll become a section that can't follow a cellist
Brass will get better on their own
Brass should not be hired young
Steady today; Saturday rehearsal with both versions; brass play both; if they can manage the breath, use it in the concert

questions.prompt: What does Janusz add on his own initiative?
options: Twenty minutes with the brass on Friday, off the clock, to talk about where he breathes and why

questions.prompt: What did the brass manage on Saturday?
options: They managed the breath at the second attempt

questions.prompt: How did the two critics describe the slow movement?
options: One called it 'unusually patient'; the other 'almost reckless in its silence'
Artistic 'honesty' depends on the people you are playing with — and a wise compromise can protect both the breath of the music and the players still learning to breathe with you.
questions.explanation: The whole shape of the dialogue.
questions.hint: The compromise and Anika's last sentence.

cloze.template:
Narrator: The Warsaw Philharmonic is rehearsing Brahms. Their new music director, Anika Müller, is thirty-eight. The principal cellist, Janusz Wójcik, is sixty-two. He has played with this orchestra for thirty years. They have stopped at the slow {{b1}}. Anika has just asked him to play 'more strictly'.
Janusz: I would like to ask, with respect, what you mean by strictly. I am not slow. I checked against the {{b2}} before rehearsal. I am within two beats per minute of the marking.
Anika: You are within the marking on the bar level. But you are stretching the second beat in every bar by almost a fifth. The audience cannot feel a {{b3}}. We sound like we are apologising for the music.
Janusz: I am stretching the second beat because the harmony asks for it. Brahms wrote this passage so that the second beat lands on the most painful {{b4}} in the movement. If I do not give that chord a moment, the listener never feels it. The metronome does not know the chord is there.
Anika: I take that point. But you cannot decide alone where to stretch. The brass behind you are following you, and they are not playing the same chord. They are playing a steady rhythm under the harmony. When you breathe, they have to choose between following you, which kills their rhythm, or ignoring you, which breaks the {{b5}}. You are putting the section in an impossible spot.
Janusz: I have played this passage in this hall under four conductors. Two of them asked me to stretch. The others did not. It can be done.
Anika: It can be done by an orchestra that already knows your stretch. The brass in 1998 had played it with you for a decade. The brass in row eight today are three {{b6}} old in this orchestra. Two of them are still finding their breath. If we do it your way, today, they will not know when to come in. Tomorrow they will know, but tomorrow is not the concert.
Janusz: So you are saying I should play less {{b7}} for the sake of newer colleagues.
Anika: I am saying you should play differently when the room is different. You played with old friends and you read each other in your sleep. You are not playing with old friends now. The orchestra has changed. The honest playing in 1998 is not the honest playing in {{b8}}.
Janusz: That is a fair sentence. But may I offer one in return? An orchestra that is afraid to breathe is not an orchestra. It is a {{b9}}. If every conductor for the next ten years protects the new brass by forbidding the breath, the brass will never learn it. They will become a section that cannot follow a cellist. That is also a kind of loss.
Anika: Then could we try this. Today, you keep the bar steady. We rehearse it without the stretch for the next two days. Then on Saturday, the day before the concert, we rehearse the same passage twice — once steady, once with your stretch — and the brass play both. We see what they can manage. If they can manage the breath, we use the breath in the {{b10}}.
Janusz: Yes. I will play it steady today. I would also like to spend twenty minutes on Friday with the brass, if they agree. Off the clock. We can talk about where I breathe and why.
Anika: Please do that. And thank you for not just doing it your way.
Janusz: Thank you for not just calling it wrong.
Narrator: On the Saturday rehearsal the brass managed the breath at the second attempt. The Sunday concert was reviewed in two newspapers. One critic called the slow movement 'unusually patient'. The other called it 'almost {{b11}} in its silence'. Neither mentioned the brass, who, Anika later said, were the people the review should have been about.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Janusz argues that an orchestra 'afraid to breathe' is just a clock. Think of a team you've been on (sports, work, school). Where did your team have to choose between strict timing and 'breath' — and what was lost when one side won?
